---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
#library(stargazer)
library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-data_blue_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, inverse

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size60[
Build the Second Report

- Section 4: Univariate Analyses (Trends)
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2024)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Make sure all data available on Canvas

2. Post Section 4 assignment details on Canvas

<br>

*Canvas*


REVISE FOR NEW DATA PROJECT




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size45[**Report 2: Analyzing our Predictor Variable(s)**]

<br>

.size40[**Section 3: What do the measures currently show us?**

Side-by-side plots (2019): Histogram and Map
- Fig 1a & 1b: Military expenditure
- Fig 2a & 2b: Current health expenditure
- Fig 3a & 3b: Government expenditure on education
]

???

### Does everybody have their figures for Section 3 finished? 

- THAT was the assignment for today!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size40[**Report 2: Analyzing our Predictor Variable(s)**]

<br>

.size40[**Section 4: How are these measures changing across time?**

Visualize the mean and median across time as a line plot:
- Fig 4: Military expenditure
- Fig 5: Current health expenditure
- Fig 6: Government expenditure on education
]

???

### Remind me, what is the difference between the mean and the median?

<br>

**SLIDE**: We need some new tools to get this done and we'll explore those tools using built-in data.




---

class: middle

.code120[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Load Package
library(tidyverse)

# View the data
population
```
]

.pull-left[
```{r}
set.seed(325)

population |>
  slice_sample(n = 10) |>
  kbl(digits = 1)
```
]

.pull-right[
.code130[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Check the range of the years
range(population$year)

# How many countries included?
unique(population$country)
```
]]

???

Let's introduce our new tools using the built-in dataset called 'population'

- I believe this is pulled from the World Health Organization which, I suspect, is pulling from the World Bank's database...

- This dataset is brought into R by the tidyr package which gets loaded when you run tidyverse

<br>

```{r, echo=TRUE}
# Check the range of the years
range(population$year)

# How many countries included?
length(unique(population$country))
```

<br>

**SLIDE**: In order to summarize the population data across the years in the dataset we'll use a new function.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size45[**Descriptive Statistics by Group**]]]

<br>

.size45[
aggregate(data, y ~ x, FUN)

- 'data' is the data set

- 'y ~ x' is how you include two variables (as a formula)

- 'FUN' is the statistic (mean, median, sd, etc.)
]

???

The aggregate function is designed to calculate a single descriptive statistic on a y variable broken into subsets by a second variable.

<br>

**SLIDE**: Let's use aggregate on our population data



---

class: middle, center

.code130[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Total World Population (per year)
aggregate(data = population, population ~ year, FUN = sum)
```
]

.pull-left[
.size25[
```{r}
aggregate(data = population, population ~ year, FUN = sum) |>
  mutate(
    population = population
  ) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  kbl(digits = 3)
```
]]

.pull-right[
.size25[
```{r}
aggregate(data = population, population ~ year, FUN = sum) |>
  mutate(
    population = population
  ) |>
  slice_tail(n = 9) |>
  kbl(digits = 3)
```
]]

???

This function uses R's formula entry.

- Here the first variable (y) is the number you want to calculate the function on

- On a pc, the tilde is the key above your tab on the left side of the keyboard

- The second variable (x) is the group you want to use for making the subsets

<br>

Here I'm using the sum function to add all the population numbers together.

<br>

### Does the code make sense?

<br>

Everybody copy and paste this code twice

- Change the first one to give us the mean per year

- Change the second to give us the median per year



---

class: middle

.pull-left[

```{r, echo=TRUE}
# Calculate mean by year
aggregate(data = population, 
          population ~ year, 
          FUN = mean)
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo=TRUE}
# Calculate median by year
aggregate(data = population, 
          population ~ year, 
          FUN = median)
```
]

???

### Does this make sense?

<br>

### Why are the means so much larger than the medians?

- (Outliers!)

<br>

**SLIDE**: Let's now use these results to build a line plot.




---

class: middle

.code100[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='60%', fig.width=6}
# Save the calculations
pop_mean <- aggregate(data = population, population ~ year, FUN = mean)
pop_median <- aggregate(data = population, population ~ year, FUN = median)

# Standard approach (One data set)
ggplot(data = pop_mean, aes(x = year, y = population)) +
  geom_line()
```
]

???

Our current approach.



---

class: middle

.code120[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='60%', fig.width=6}
# Move the details to the geom line
ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = pop_mean, aes(x = year, y = population)) #<<
```
]

???

First new lesson for us

- You can put all the pieces for the plot in the geom line instead of in the ggplot

<br>

**SLIDE**: Doing this allows you to add more geoms to the plot AND to give each one their own data set



---

class: middle

.code120[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='60%', fig.width=6}
# Two geoms, two data sets
ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = pop_mean, aes(x = year, y = population)) +
  geom_line(data = pop_median, aes(x = year, y = population)) #<<
```
]

???

### Make sense?

<br>

**SLIDE**: Let's now distinguish between the two lines.



---

class: middle

.code110[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='60%', fig.width=6}
# Add colors
ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = pop_mean, aes(x = year, y = population), color = "red") + #<<
  geom_line(data = pop_median, aes(x = year, y = population), color = "blue") #<<
```
]

???

Add colors



---

class: middle

.code100[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='60%', fig.width=6}
# Annotate
ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = pop_mean, aes(x = year, y = population), color = "red") +
  geom_line(data = pop_median, aes(x = year, y = population), color = "blue") +
  annotate("text", x = 2012, y = 3e7, label = "Mean", color = "red") + #<<
  annotate("text", x = 2012, y = 1e7, label = "Median", color = "blue") #<<
```
]

???

Add text with the annotate function

- Coordinates on the plot using x and y arguments

- The label argument is what word you want written

### Questions on the code?

<br>

**SLIDE**: Let's make a few more tweaks!




---

class: middle

.code100[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='60%', fig.width=6}
ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = pop_mean, aes(x = year, y = population), color = "red") +
  geom_line(data = pop_median, aes(x = year, y = population), color = "blue") +
  annotate("text", x = 2012, y = 3e7, label = "Mean", color = "red") +
  annotate("text", x = 2012, y = 1e7, label = "Median", color = "blue") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1995, 2013, 2)) + #<<
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = c(1e7, 2e7, 3e7), labels = c("10", "20", "30")) + #<<
  labs(x = "", y = "Global Population (millions)") + #<<
  theme_bw() #<<
```
]

???





### Remind me, what does it tell us that the mean and median for population so different from each other here?

- (Outliers!)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size40[**Report 2: Analyzing our Predictor Variable(s)**]

<br>

.size40[**Section 4: How are these measures changing across time?**

Visualize the mean and median across time as a line plot:
- Fig 4: Military expenditure
- Fig 5: Current health expenditure
- Fig 6: Government expenditure on education
]

???

I don't mean to imply that this is a better way to perform univariate analyses across time.

- I think the across time maps you made for report 1 were super handy for helping us identify regional variations in the data.

- I simply want to give you another set of tools for analyzing data across time!

<br>

Ok, let's practice this new code by building these plots today!












---

APPLIED TO WDI



---

```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='60%', fig.width=6, eval=FALSE}
d <- read_excel("../Project-SP23/PREDICTOR-Government_Spending/Project_Predictors-WDI-2008_2020.xlsx", na = "NA")

military_mean <- aggregate(data = d, military ~ year, FUN = mean)
military_median <- aggregate(data = d, military ~ year, FUN = median)

# Build up to plotting both
ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = military_mean, aes(x = year, y = military), color = "red") + #<<
  geom_line(data = military_median, aes(x = year, y = military), color = "blue") #<<
```


---

```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='60%', fig.width=6, eval=FALSE}
education_mean <- aggregate(data = d, education ~ year, FUN = mean)
education_median <- aggregate(data = d, education ~ year, FUN = median)

# Build up to plotting both
ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = education_mean, aes(x = year, y = education), color = "red") + #<<
  geom_line(data = education_median, aes(x = year, y = education), color = "blue") #<<
```




---

```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, out.width='60%', fig.width=6, eval=FALSE}
health_mean <- aggregate(data = d, health ~ year, FUN = mean)
health_median <- aggregate(data = d, health ~ year, FUN = median)

# Build up to plotting both
ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = health_mean, aes(x = year, y = health), color = "red") + #<<
  geom_line(data = health_median, aes(x = year, y = health), color = "blue") #<<
```



---

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
# Savannah questions on mapping
wdi_2019 <- filter(d, year == 2019)

military_2019_map <- rworldmap::joinCountryData2Map(dF= wdi_2019, joinCode = "ISO3", nameJoinColumn = "country_code")

mapCountryData(military_2019_map, 
               nameColumnToPlot = "military", 
               colourPalette = c("#FCCDE5", "#E5f5E0", "#A1D99B", "#31A354"), 
               mapTitle= "Global Military Expenditures As % of GDP (2019)", 
               catMethod= c(.1394, 1.0186, 1.4326, 2.1743, 8.6377))

#theme(plot.title = element_text(size = rel(2), family = "serif"))

# Test exporting data manually


png("Test1.png", width = 600, height = 400)
mapCountryData(military_2019_map, 
               nameColumnToPlot = "military", 
               colourPalette = c("#FCCDE5", "#E5f5E0", "#A1D99B", "#31A354"), 
               mapTitle= "Global Military Expenditures As % of GDP (2019)", 
               catMethod= c(.1394, 1.0186, 1.4326, 2.1743, 8.6377))
dev.off()


library(svglite)
svglite("Test1.svg", width = 8, height = 5)
mapCountryData(military_2019_map, 
               nameColumnToPlot = "military", 
               colourPalette = c("#FCCDE5", "#E5f5E0", "#A1D99B", "#31A354"), 
               mapTitle= "Global Military Expenditures As % of GDP (2019)", 
               catMethod= c(.1394, 1.0186, 1.4326, 2.1743, 8.6377))
dev.off()



```
